title: Rome
summary: The city on the Tiber that ruled an empire, became the seat of a universal Church, was sacked and rebuilt multiple times, and remains the most continuously symbolically contested location in the Western world — a place that every subsequent civilization has claimed, imitated, mourned, or defined itself against.
hook: Rome is not a city that had an empire. Rome is an idea that has been using a city as its address for three thousand years. The Caesars, the Popes, Mussolini, and the European Union have all claimed Rome as their origin or their authority. The ruins are not ruins — they are arguments, still active.

beginner.thePlace:
Rome is not primarily a city. It is an idea that has been using a city as its address for three thousand years. The Caesars, the Popes, Mussolini, and the framers of the American Constitution have all claimed Rome as their origin story and their legitimation.

The city sits on seven hills above the Tiber River, about twenty kilometers from the sea. It has been continuously inhabited for three thousand years. Capital of a republic. Center of a world empire. Seat of a church with over a billion followers. Modern city of four million people. Every phase left something behind, built into the walls.
beginner.physicalWorld:
At its imperial peak, Rome held perhaps a million people — a number not matched anywhere in Western Europe until London in the 1800s. The city had to be fed, which meant grain ships arriving constantly from Egypt and North Africa.

The Rome that tourists visit is the Rome of the powerful — temples, forums, amphitheaters. Most ordinary Roman buildings are gone. The hills of Rome are real hills, steep enough that climbing them was work. Building the city required draining wetlands, quarrying stone, and bringing water in through aqueducts from distant springs.
beginner.theLayers:
When archaeologists dig anywhere in Rome, they find layers — buildings built on ruins of earlier buildings, streets laid over older streets. Every century added something and buried something else.

The Colosseum was built in the first century CE as an entertainment venue. Over the following centuries it became a quarry, a fortress, a Christian shrine, and eventually a monument. The Forum Romanum — the heart of the ancient city — was buried under centuries of debris and forgotten until excavations beginning in the 1700s slowly exposed it. What you see today reflects decisions about which layers to reveal and which to leave buried.
beginner.whatItBecame: Rome became the most symbolically powerful city in Western civilization through three transformations. First, it became the center of an empire — Roman law, Latin, and Roman roads spread across territory from Scotland to Mesopotamia. Second, it became the center of the Christian church: the authority of the papacy was built on the tradition that Peter was martyred in Rome, and churches replaced temples. Third, it became the model: every empire and republic that came after positioned itself in relation to Rome. The rulers who called themselves Kaiser or Tsar were using forms of the word Caesar. When the American founders designed their government, they borrowed Roman vocabulary: senate, republic, capital, forum.
beginner.whoClaimsIt:
The Catholic Church claims Rome as the seat of papal authority — the Vatican is a city within the city, governing more than a billion Catholics worldwide. The Italian Republic claims it as its capital. Italy did not exist as a unified country until 1871, so making Rome the capital was a deliberate choice about which history the new nation wanted to inherit.

There is also an eastern claim Western history often forgets. When Constantinople fell in 1453, the Russian state called Moscow the Third Rome — the true continuation of the Roman tradition. That claim shaped Russian political identity for centuries. Less formally, Rome is claimed by everyone whose legal systems, architectural vocabulary, and political institutions derive from what it produced.
beginner.theLongLife:
Rome has been dying and surviving for two thousand years. The Western Roman Empire collapsed in the fifth century. Rome the city kept going. Rome the idea kept going more persistently still. Every generation has found a use for the Roman inheritance — as a model, a standard, a warning, or a source of authority.

The physical city makes these claims possible in a way almost no other place can. You can go to Rome, stand in the Colosseum, walk through the Forum. The actual stones are still there. It is not a ruin that was once great and is now only a memory. It is a city that has been continuously, imperfectly inhabited for three thousand years. The past is not behind Rome. It is Rome.

general.thePlace:
Rome sits on seven hills above a bend in the Tiber River, approximately twenty kilometers from the Tyrrhenian Sea. The location was militarily and commercially strategic — defensible hills, a river crossing, access to the sea — but not exceptional. Dozens of other sites in Italy had comparable natural advantages. What made Rome Rome was not geography but history: the accumulation of power, population, monuments, and meaning over three thousand years.

The city today has a population of approximately four million. It is simultaneously an ancient site, a medieval city, a Renaissance capital, a Baroque creation, a nineteenth-century national capital, and a twenty-first-century European metropolis. These layers coexist imperfectly — Roman ruins interrupt traffic patterns, medieval churches anchor Renaissance piazzas, ancient stones are repurposed in building after building. The experience of the city is palimpsestic — layered, like a manuscript written over and over — every surface has writing under writing.
general.physicalWorld:
The physical Rome that shaped history was a city of hills and valleys, of quarries and aqueducts, of swamps drained and floods managed. The Tiber flooded regularly until modern embankments were built in the nineteenth century. The Roman hills — Palatine, Capitoline, Aventine, and the rest — structured social geography. The elite lived on the Palatine (from which 'palace' derives); the commercial population occupied the valleys below.

Rome's physical scale at its imperial peak was extraordinary. A city of perhaps one million inhabitants — not exceeded in the Western world until London in the nineteenth century. Fed by grain from Egypt and North Africa. Supplied by eleven aqueducts. Sheltered in multi-story apartment buildings (insulae) that were the ancient world's equivalent of modern urban housing.
general.theLayers:
The archaeological layers of Rome span three thousand years. Republican temples, Imperial forums, early Christian basilicas, medieval towers, Renaissance palaces, Baroque fountains, and Fascist boulevards occupy the same urban territory. Some layers are visible, some buried, some preserved only in documents. The Colosseum — a Flavian amphitheater of the first century CE — was subsequently used as a quarry, a fortress, a lime kiln, and a Christian sacred site before becoming a tourist monument. No Roman site exists in a single temporal moment.

The Forum Romanum — the civic center of the Republic and early Empire — was buried under medieval accumulation until Enlightenment and nineteenth-century excavation exposed it. What visitors see today is a reconstruction of an excavation — a set of choices about which layers to preserve and which to remove. Every archaeological site is an argument about which past matters.
general.whatItBecame:
Rome became the most powerful symbolic site in Western civilization through three successive transformations. First, it became the center of a world empire — the city that gave laws, language, and infrastructure to the Mediterranean world. Second, it became the seat of the Catholic Church — the city where Peter was martyred, where the papacy established itself, where Christian authority was grounded in apostolic succession. Third, it became the model for all subsequent empires and republics — the reference point against which European political ambitions defined themselves.

What was erased in each transformation is as important as what was preserved. The Republican city was partially erased by the Imperial transformation. The pagan city was systematically converted by Christian Rome — temples became churches, sacred sites were reconsecrated, pagan memory was suppressed. The medieval and Renaissance city was partially demolished for Fascist boulevards in the 1930s. Mussolini sought a visual connection to Imperial Rome — excavating ancient monuments while destroying the medieval layers around them.

The symbolic weight of Rome has always required someone to be forgotten. Each claimant to the Roman inheritance has defined the Rome they want against the Rome they cannot use.
general.whoClaimsIt:
The claims on Rome are still active. The Catholic Church claims it as the seat of apostolic authority — the city where Peter and Paul were martyred, where the papacy is grounded. The Italian Republic claims it as its capital, inheriting the republican tradition. The European Union gestures toward it as the site of the Treaty of Rome (1957), the founding document of European integration.

Less official claims are equally powerful. Rome has been claimed as ancestor of Western law, Western architecture, Western Christianity, and Western political thought — each claim grounded in specific Roman institutions and practices. These claims are taught, contested, and revised continuously in universities, museums, and political arguments.

The eastern inheritance — Byzantium as the continuation of Rome, Constantinople as the 'Second Rome,' Moscow as the 'Third Rome' — represents a separate but parallel tradition of Roman claim-making that Western historiography often underweights.
general.theLongLife:
Rome's long life as a symbolic site has produced a continuous tradition of what scholars call translatio imperii — the transfer of imperial authority. The medieval Holy Roman Empire claimed Roman succession. Charlemagne was crowned in Rome. Napoleon claimed Roman symbolism. Mussolini explicitly sought to revive Roman grandeur. The United States Capitol's architectural vocabulary — domes, columns, Latin inscriptions — is a Roman claim made in neoclassical code.

The city's physical presence has always anchored these symbolic claims. You can go to Rome. You can stand in the Colosseum, touch the stones of the Forum, walk through Hadrian's Pantheon. The physical continuity of the city — still inhabited, still building, still accumulating new layers — gives the symbolic claims a material grounding that imagined communities cannot match. Rome is not just an idea. It is also a place where the idea has been enacted, continuously, for three thousand years.

advanced.thePlace: The concept of the 'eternal city' — Roma aeterna — is itself a historical construction with a specific political genealogy. The phrase enters Latin literature during the Augustan period, when imperial ideology required the city to be understood as permanent and divinely sanctioned, not as one successful polity among many. Mary Beard's SPQR (2015) is the essential corrective to the eternal city mythology: Beard argues that Rome's success was contingent, its institutions improvised rather than planned, and that the story Romans told about their own origins was almost entirely invented in retrospect. The gap between Rome as it was and Rome as it has been remembered is itself the entry's central analytical problem — and that gap was first opened by Romans themselves.
advanced.physicalWorld: The archaeology of Rome is not politically neutral. The most significant 20th-century intervention in the physical city was Mussolini's excavation program of the 1920s and 1930s, which demolished substantial portions of the medieval and Renaissance city to expose ancient monuments that could then be used to establish visual continuity between the Fascist state and Imperial Rome. The Via dell'Impero — now the Via dei Fori Imperiali — was cut through the Forum area, destroying significant archaeological strata in order to create a boulevard suitable for military parades past ancient ruins. Richard Brilliant and other archaeologists have analyzed how this destruction was ideologically purposeful: the medieval layers that connected Rome to Christianity and the papacy were inconvenient for a regime seeking pagan imperial precedent. Every archaeological site is an argument about which past matters; the Rome that tourists visit is partly Mussolini's argument.
advanced.theLayers: The historiography of Rome's decline is one of the longest-running debates in Western historical scholarship, with direct implications for how subsequent civilizations understand their own vulnerability. Gibbon's The History of the Decline and Fall of the Roman Empire (1776–1789) established the Christianity thesis — that Rome was weakened from within by a religion that redirected civic virtue toward otherworldly salvation — and the barbarian invasion thesis. Both have been substantially revised. Bryan Ward-Perkins's The Fall of Rome and the End of Civilization (2005) argues, against the revisionist tendency to see 'transformation' rather than 'collapse,' that the material evidence — declining pottery production, shrinking building sizes, loss of long-distance trade — demonstrates genuine civilizational regression. Peter Heather's work emphasizes external pressure from Gothic and Hunnic peoples rather than internal decay. The contemporary institutional economics approach (Acemoglu, Robinson) treats Rome's fall as a consequence of elite extraction and institutional degradation that the empire could not reverse — a case study in how extractive institutions eventually undermine the civilizations that build them.
advanced.whatItBecame: The use of Roman precedent by Fascist movements represents the paradigm case of political appropriation — and the most analytically revealing one because it demonstrates how completely the symbolic content of a historical precedent can be separated from its historical reality. Mussolini's Rome was largely invented: the Roman Empire was multiethnic and multiracial by any modern standard; it extended citizenship progressively to conquered peoples; its economy was organized around trade and taxation rather than racial hierarchy. The Fascist Rome that Mussolini constructed required suppressing or ignoring virtually everything that made actual Roman history interesting. Roger Griffin's analysis of generic fascism as a 'palingenetic ultranationalist' ideology — centered on the myth of national rebirth from decadence — shows why Rome was structurally attractive to fascism regardless of historical accuracy: what mattered was the myth of imperial greatness that could be recovered, not the actual institutional content of Roman governance.
advanced.whoClaimsIt: The American founders' use of Roman precedent has been analyzed most rigorously by Carl Richard's The Founders and the Classics (1994) and Eran Shalev's Rome Reborn on Western Shores (2009). Both argue that the founders used Rome selectively and sometimes anachronistically — reading the Roman republic through Enlightenment political theory rather than through Roman sources directly. The pseudonym 'Publius' that Hamilton, Madison, and Jay used for the Federalist Papers honored a founder of the Republic; but the Federalist Papers' analysis of Roman institutions drew more on Montesquieu and Polybius than on firsthand engagement with Roman historical evidence. The founders needed Rome to demonstrate that republican government was possible in large, commercial societies — a point the historical record only partially supports, and which they partially manufactured by reading Roman history through their preferred conclusion.
advanced.theLongLife: The comparative question — why Rome specifically has sustained symbolic reinvention across more than two thousand years, when other great ancient civilizations have not — has been addressed most directly by scholars working at the intersection of memory studies and political theory. Jan Assmann, a German Egyptologist and cultural theorist, distinguishes in his theory of cultural memory between 'communicative memory' (within living memory, up to about three generations) and 'cultural memory' (the organized, institutionalized memory of founding events and figures that defines collective identity). Rome has sustained cultural memory at this intensity because three distinct institutional systems — the Catholic Church, Roman law, and the humanist educational tradition — continuously transmitted, curated, and rearticulated Roman precedent across every subsequent century. Without the Church, Roman law would be an archaeological curiosity; without the humanist tradition, it would not have shaped political imagination. The question of Rome's long life is partly a question about which institutions were sufficiently robust to carry the memory forward.

foundation.theStory: Rome is not primarily a place — it is an argument about civilization that has been made from the same physical location for three thousand years. The city on the Tiber has been claimed by Roman emperors, Catholic popes, Renaissance humanists, Enlightenment philosophers, nineteenth-century nationalists, Fascist dictators, and European federalists. Each claim required a version of Rome: which layers to excavate, which to preserve, which to destroy. Understanding Rome means understanding how physical places become symbolic resources.
foundation.theDebate: The central debates concern which Rome counts: the Republic or the Empire, pagan Rome or Christian Rome, the eternal city of the tourist imagination or the inhabited city of four million people. The broader question — how places acquire and maintain symbolic meaning across radically different political and cultural contexts — is one of the foundational questions of cultural history.
foundation.whyItMatters: Rome matters as the most continuously symbolically contested location in the Western world. Every subsequent civilization that has claimed Mediterranean or Western authority has had to define its relationship to Rome — through imitation, rejection, or appropriation. Understanding how Rome has been claimed and reclaimed across three thousand years is essential for understanding how symbolic authority works in Western political culture.
questions: Each claimant to Rome — emperors, popes, humanists, nationalists, Fascists — selected different aspects of Roman history as their inheritance while suppressing others. What does this selective appropriation reveal about the relationship between historical memory and political authority?

Mussolini demolished medieval and Renaissance Rome to expose the ancient monuments and create visual connections to Imperial Rome. What is the relationship between archaeological excavation and political ideology? Can the desire to recover the past ever be fully separated from the desire to use the past?

interpretation.scholarlyConversation: Rome scholarship divides between classicists and archaeologists focused on the ancient city (Beard, Coarelli), art historians and architectural historians focused on medieval and Renaissance Rome (Krautheimer, Stinger), historians of late antiquity focused on the Christian transformation (Brown, Krautheimer), and cultural historians focused on Rome's symbolic afterlife (Edwards, Hughes). The field is vast; no single synthesis covers all periods adequately.
interpretation.evidenceAndLimits: The physical evidence for Rome across three thousand years is exceptionally rich — continuous habitation means continuous archaeological deposition, and the city has been studied intensively since the Renaissance. The limits: continuous habitation also means continuous destruction. Each generation's building has damaged or destroyed earlier remains. The 'complete' Rome is always a reconstruction; what survives is a sample shaped by accident, politics, and successive decisions about which past to preserve.
interpretation.takingAPosition: A strong scholarly argument about Rome must specify which Rome is being analyzed — which period, which population, which symbolic tradition. Arguments that treat 'Rome' as a unified subject across three thousand years risk confusing radically different historical situations. The most useful scholarly work on Rome tends to focus on specific transitions: how Rome became Christian, how Renaissance humanists constructed their version of antiquity, how nineteenth-century nationalism used Roman imagery.
questions: The phrase 'all roads lead to Rome' encodes both a historical fact (the Roman road system) and a cultural claim (the centrality of Rome to Western civilization). How do material facts become metaphors? What is the relationship between Rome's actual geographic centrality in the ancient world and its symbolic centrality in subsequent cultures?

Rome has been claimed by the Catholic Church, by Italian nationalism, by European federalism, and by American republicanism. How can a single place support such different claims? What does the multiplicity of Roman inheritances suggest about how historical precedents function in political argument?

research.topic: The population of ancient Rome at its imperial peak
research.content: Demographic historians estimate that Rome's population reached approximately one million at its peak in the first and second centuries CE, making it the largest city in the Western world until London reached comparable size in the nineteenth century. The estimate derives from the carrying capacity of the grain dole system (which fed several hundred thousand people), the documented extent of the city's infrastructure (eleven aqueducts capable of delivering vast quantities of water), and comparison with other ancient sources. The estimate is not universally accepted but has broad scholarly support.

research.topic: The Fascist transformation of Rome's urban fabric
research.content: Historical scholarship has documented that Mussolini's regime deliberately demolished medieval and Renaissance urban fabric surrounding ancient Roman monuments in the 1930s — creating the Via dei Fori Imperiali and Via della Conciliazione, among other interventions. The demolition was ideologically motivated: the regime sought to create visual connections between the Fascist state and Imperial Rome by removing the intervening centuries. The lost buildings and urban fabric are documented in archival photographs and historical surveys. The intervention transformed how the ancient monuments are experienced and what urban context surrounds them.

research.topic: The daily experience of Rome's non-elite inhabitants in the imperial period
research.content: The experience of the hundreds of thousands of ordinary Romans — the apartment-dwelling workers, slaves, freedmen, and immigrants who made up most of the city's population — cannot be recovered in detail. What evidence exists: the multi-story insulae whose ruins survive in Ostia (Rome's port city), graffiti from Pompeii that provide glimpses of working-class life, legal texts that regulated apartment tenancy and street conduct. What cannot be recovered: the daily texture of life in the city's lower districts, the experience of the city's enormous slave population, and the perspectives of the immigrants from across the empire who made Rome the cosmopolitan city it was.

comparativeNarrative.name: Byzantine and Eastern Perspective
comparativeNarrative.content: The Eastern Roman Empire — Byzantium — considered itself the continuation of Rome, not its successor. Constantinople was the 'New Rome,' built by Constantine as a second capital; after Rome's fall in 476 CE, Constantinople was the Roman Empire's capital for another thousand years. The concept of 'Third Rome' — Moscow as the heir of Byzantium — extended this tradition further east. The Western historiographical focus on Rome as the origin of European civilization often obscures this parallel tradition of Roman claim-making in the Orthodox Christian world.

comparativeNarrative.name: Conquered Peoples' Perspective
comparativeNarrative.content: For the peoples Rome conquered — Gauls, Britons, Jews, Carthaginians, Iberians — Rome was not a symbol of civilization but of conquest, enslavement, and cultural destruction. The Jewish experience of Rome — the destruction of the Temple in 70 CE, the diaspora that followed — is the starkest example of Rome's conquest meaning cultural catastrophe rather than civilizational transmission. The long history of Western civilization's celebration of Rome has embedded this particular victor's narrative so deeply that the perspectives of the conquered are only recently being systematically recovered.

comparativeNarrative.name: Contemporary Roman Perspective
comparativeNarrative.content: For the four million people who live in Rome, the ancient city is simultaneously a heritage asset, a tourist economy, a traffic impediment, and a daily presence. Romans live on top of history in ways that are sometimes inconvenient: underground subway construction is regularly halted by archaeological discoveries; buildings cannot be demolished without archaeological survey; entire neighborhoods are protected by heritage regulations that constrain urban development. The lived experience of Rome's inhabitants involves a relationship to the past that is less reverential and more practical than the tourist or scholarly perspective.

rabbitHole.label: The Roman Empire
rabbitHole.reason: The Roman Empire was the political and administrative system that made Rome the capital of the Mediterranean world — understanding the empire is essential for understanding the city's historical significance.

rabbitHole.label: The Roman Republic
rabbitHole.reason: The Roman Republic produced the political forms — Senate, consuls, citizenship, law — that subsequent civilizations claimed as their inheritance.

rabbitHole.label: Christianity
rabbitHole.reason: Rome's transformation from the capital of a pagan empire to the seat of the Catholic Church is the city's defining religious transformation — and the source of its most enduring claim on Western civilization.

rabbitHole.label: The Renaissance
rabbitHole.reason: Rome was the physical site of the Renaissance's encounter with antiquity — the ruins that inspired Petrarch, the ancient buildings that shaped Brunelleschi, the collections that defined humanist learning.

rabbitHole.label: Petrarch
rabbitHole.reason: Petrarch's invention of historical consciousness — the sense that Rome was a lost world to be mourned and recovered — made the physical ruins of Rome symbolically potent in a new way.

reference.author: Beard, Mary
reference.title: SPQR: A History of Ancient Rome
reference.annotation: The essential scholarly account of Rome's republican and imperial history — Beard's approach foregrounds ordinary Romans rather than emperors and asks what Rome's history means for contemporary politics and identity.

reference.author: Krautheimer, Richard
reference.title: Rome: Profile of a City, 312–1308
reference.annotation: The foundational account of medieval Rome — Krautheimer's architectural and urban history traces how Christian Rome was built on and around the pagan city, and how the Church used architecture to construct its claims.

reference.author: Edwards, Catharine
reference.title: Writing Rome: Textual Approaches to the City
reference.annotation: The essential account of how Rome has been written — Edwards traces how ancient and modern authors have constructed textual Romes that serve their own purposes, making the city's symbolic history visible.

reference.author: Hughes, Robert
reference.title: Rome
reference.annotation: The best accessible narrative — Hughes's history of Rome for general readers is richly illustrated, elegantly written, and covers the city from antiquity through the twentieth century.

reference.author: Brown, Peter
reference.title: The Rise of Western Christendom
reference.annotation: The essential account of how Christianity transformed Rome — Brown's synthesis of late antiquity shows how the city became the seat of a universal Church and what that transformation meant for European civilization.

reference.author: Coarelli, Filippo
reference.title: Rome and Environs: An Archaeological Guide
reference.annotation: The authoritative archaeological reference — Coarelli's guide to Rome's archaeological sites is the essential companion for anyone who wants to understand the physical city rather than just its symbolic history.

commerce.title: SPQR: A History of Ancient Rome
commerce.author: Mary Beard
commerce.note: Start here — Beard's history is scholarly, readable, and asks the right questions about what Rome's history means rather than just narrating events.

commerce.title: Rome
commerce.author: Robert Hughes
commerce.note: The best accessible narrative — Hughes covers the full sweep of Rome's history from antiquity through the twentieth century with a critic's eye for the visual and the symbolic.

commerce.title: Writing Rome
commerce.author: Catharine Edwards
commerce.note: Essential for the symbolic history — Edwards traces how Rome has been constructed in text across antiquity and modernity, making the city's claim-making visible.

commerce.title: The Rise of Western Christendom
commerce.author: Peter Brown
commerce.note: Essential for the religious transformation — Brown shows how Rome became the seat of a universal Church and what that meant for the city's subsequent history.

commerce.title: Rome: Profile of a City, 312–1308
commerce.author: Richard Krautheimer
commerce.note: The foundational account of medieval Rome — essential for understanding how Christian Rome was built on and around the pagan city.

commerce.title: Rome and Environs: An Archaeological Guide
commerce.author: Filippo Coarelli
commerce.note: The authoritative archaeological reference — for readers who want to understand the physical city rather than just its symbolic history.

popularCulture.title: Roman Holiday (1953) / La Dolce Vita (1960)
popularCulture.description: These two films represent Rome's two dominant popular registers: the eternal romantic city of monuments, fountains, and tourist enchantment (Roman Holiday), and the decadent, restless, spiritually empty modern city beneath the ancient surface (Fellini's La Dolce Vita). Both are myths, but they are myths about real things — about what Rome does to the imagination and what it conceals.

popularCulture.title: 'All roads lead to Rome' and Roman infrastructure metaphors
popularCulture.description: Roman infrastructure metaphors — roads, aqueducts, the Colosseum, the gladiatorial arena — have entered everyday language and political discourse as shorthand for imperial ambition, durable construction, and brutal spectacle. The phrase 'all roads lead to Rome' encodes both the actual Roman road system and a claim about cultural centrality. These metaphors are so embedded in English usage that they function as arguments about power and civilization even when unrecognized as Roman.

popularCulture.title: Rome (HBO, 2005–2007)
popularCulture.description: HBO's Rome depicted the late Republic through the eyes of two ordinary soldiers — a dramaturgical choice that tried to give the political history human scale. Expensive, carefully researched, and ultimately cancelled for cost reasons, it represents the most ambitious attempt to bring Republican Rome to television. Its depiction of Rome as a city of violence, sexuality, and class conflict was more accurate than the toga-drama tradition it replaced.

popularCulture.title: Rome as mirror for American empire
popularCulture.description: The comparison between Rome and the United States has been a staple of American political commentary since the founding — the Founders consciously modeled American institutions on Roman Republican forms. The comparison intensified with American global power after 1945, generating a genre of 'decline of Rome as warning for America' books. The comparison is illuminating and misleading simultaneously: the structural parallels are real, but the differences between ancient and modern imperial power are equally significant.

popularCulture.title: Total War: Rome (2004 and sequels)
popularCulture.description: The Total War: Rome strategy game series has introduced millions of players to the Roman world through the mechanics of military conquest and empire management. The games are not history, but they embed players in the logic of Roman expansion in ways that make the empire's structure experientially comprehensible. They are probably the most widely played encounter with Roman history in the contemporary world.
